description: LLOYD GEORGE TO FACE HIS CRITICS Continued from Page One impinged on the special powers which ho had been granted by an Order in Council soon after he took up his posi tion as Chief of the Imperial General Staff It may be remembered that when he took office he made it a con dition that he should have direct ac cess to the Cabinet and issue orders over his name to commander in the field instead of their going through the Army Council for discussion or di rection or even the Secretary of State for War The system was considered to be of military advantage at the time as facilitating rapidity of action So that Robertson acted directly under the War Cabinet and not under the Secretary of State fo War The system while contrary to theAradi tions of British policy and democratic Government has been accepted well up to now but the enlarged powers recent ly given to the Supreme War Council placing executive authority in the hands of the military staff attached to it for certain functions made a modification of the Order in Council necessary Robertson felt that such a modifica tion would lessen his authority
